# Literature Review
Cancer mutation patters are a well studied domain. Large pan-cancer studies have shown that mutation patterns vary substantially across tumor types and remain strongly structured by tissue and cell of origin. The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) Pan-Cancer Analysis Project established the value of comparing tumors across cancer types of identify both shared and specific genomic features, and later analyses of roughly 10,000 tumors across 33 cancers found that molecular clustering is organized primarily by histology, tissue type, and anatomic origin (Weinstein et al., 2013; Hoadley et al., 2018). These findings suggest that mutation prevalence should not be treated as either fully pooled across cancers or fully unrelated across cancers. Instead, models should allow for information sharing across cancer types. A challenge is that mutation rates are not directly comparable. Lawrence et al. (2013) showed that the search for cancer associated genes is complicated by mutational heterogeneity across genes. 

Bayesian and hierarchical models have been used extensively in cancer genomics. In a recent large-scale study of RAS mutations across human cancers, Scharpf et al. (2022) used Bayesian hierarchical models to estimate cancer-specific mutation prevalence, showing that prevalence and comutation structure are strongly context dependent. Samorodnitsky et al. (2020) also developed a Bayesian hierarchical model for the effect of somatic mutations on survival, motivated by the idea that mutation effects may be similar, but not identical, across cancer types. These studies support the use of hierarchical Bayesian modeling in pan-cancer studies.  

We build on well-established hierarchical Bayesian methods in a simplified and interpretable setting. By modeling mutation counts using a hierarchical Beta-Binomial structure and focusing on cross-cancer variation within genes, the model is a middle ground between independent estimates and interaction-based approaches.
